title: 1981-04-16_Pat_182.txt
key_claims: The reading of the narrative by Hagia Sophia explains Malebranche's assertion that "we see all things in God." We don't see an objective world; we experience the narrative Hagia Sophia chooses to read to us. She is the *Pantocratrix*, possessing the power to create and abolish the world.

Dick connects the new co-dominant ape species (the Nearman from his vision) to the figure of Christ. The Nearman is the first entity to internalize the universal Forms (*eide*) and use them to build a mental analog of reality.

Because the Nearman can abstract reality, he breaks the absolute power of the narrative (Heimarmene). This cognitive liberation allows him to "trap" the narrative and foresee it through *anamnesis* (remembering it before it happens).

Thus, the birth of Christ is equated with this primordial event of human cognitive liberation. In 3-74, Dick acted out this Nearman/Christ role, remembering the script via anamnesis and cleverly outwitting the deterministic trap set by the Xerox missive.
recurring_concepts: **Heimarmene as Script**: Deterministic fate is simply the fact that the universe is a pre-written text currently being read aloud.
reading_excerpt: The reading of the narrative by Hagia Sophia explains Malebranche's assertion that "we see all things in God." We don't see an objective world; we experience the narrative Hagia Sophia chooses to read to us. She is the *Pantocratrix*, possessing the power to create and abolish the world.
